text: A 30-degree scope is inserted, then the inferior port is planned, fashioned along the axis of the hilum, opposite the highest point in the dome of the diaphragm.
Releasing the inferior pulmonary ligament makes a very good starting point.
It's best to start with the easiest group and build up the level of complexity as you go along.
It is imperative to know the surrounding anatomy in extreme detail and identify caveats and potential areas of danger.
Our method of dissection evolved into using a malleable diatherm spatula, insulated albeit for the last 1 or 2 mm, whilst keeping the energy level at low.

Releasing the inferior pulmonary ligament exposes the station 9 nodes around the inferior pulmonary vein, and further opening of the pleura between the inferior cava and the oesophagus exposes the station 8 nodes.
The number and consistency of nodes vary greatly here.
Nodes could be absent, discrete, or form a dense fibro fatty block amenable to unblocked dissection.
In the right chest, the subcrinal space is accessed between the right main bronchus and the ozygous vein.
One should not worry too much about injuring the thoracic duct as it is tucked away between the oesophagus and the vertebral bodies out of harm's way.
Dissection of this group is started by opening the pleural reflexion at the back of the hilum from the inferior ligament up to the concavity of the ozygous vein.
The vagus could be retracted using a proline sling, but that is optional for ease of dissection.
The subcarinal nerves are dissected of their blood supply, taking care not to dig holes in the membranous part of the bronchus or the delicate oesophagus.
Dissection is carried upwards until the carina and the left main bronchus are clearly identified.
Care must be exercised in labeling nodes appropriately, as parasophageal station 8 and parabronchial station 10 are easily mislabeled as station 7.
By the end of this dissection, the left main bronchus,
oesophagus and the back of the pericardium are clearly identified and cleared of all nodal tissue.
Dissection of the superior mediastinal fibrofatty nodal tissue should be attempted unblocked.
This block of tissue can be accessed within what I call here the superior triangle.
The triangle is limited by two nerves, the vagus and the phrenic, and based on the ozygous vein.
A trapdoor is opened in the mediastinal pleura to expose the fibrofatty nodal block.
Once this is completed, the vagus could be identified stuck to the inside of the pleural reflection.
Slinging the vagus and the pleura over the superior vena cava can be helpful in opening the space.
Dissection is started at the border of the superior vena cava by pushing the superior vena cava away from the block.
One should be aware of the existence of at least one constant vein that drains directly into the SVC.
It is recommended to control this draining vein by lager clips before division.
If accidentally cut, these veins have the propensity to retract and disappear, making control of bleeding difficult.
Dissection is then deepened until the main stem trachea is identified.
Dissection is continued using pinpoint diathermy and the apex of the block is brought down off the main stem trachea.
The precarinal station 3 nodes on the right side are considered to be N2 nodes and hence the dissected block of tissue should be labeled as stations 2 to 4.
The block is then delivered in a polythene bag for ease of retrieval, but this is not necessary for individual small nodes.
After removal of the block,
the bed of the superior triangle comes into light.

The superior vena cava with the phrenic nerve plastered to its lateral pleural surface forms the right border of the triangle, whereas the vagus, also plastered to the pleural reflection, forms the left border of the triangle.
Between the two, the arch of the aorta and the origin of the right brachiocephalic artery are noted.
Dissection at the apex of the triangle should avoid diathermy around the point of division of the brachiocephalic artery.
as this is the likely location where the recurrent laryngeal nerve could be encountered.
The entire length of the main stem trachea is laid bare of nodes and the subcoronary space is checked one last time before ending the procedure.
In the left chest, systematic nodal dissection is restarted similarly by releasing the inferior pulmonary ligament.
Station 9 nodes are found within the ligament and around the inferior pulmonary vein.
Dissection in these crevices is held by retraction of the diaphragm and sometimes pushing on the pericardium.
The medial medistinal pleura is opened, as before, to expose the parasophageal nodes, station 8, care being taken not to injure the oesophagus or the vagus nerve.
Again, the number and consistency of the fibrofatty nodal tissue may vary greatly here.
Nodes may be completely absent, discrete, or contained within a dense block of fatty tissue.
Removal of the subcrinal nodes, station 7 on the left side, is the most taxing and time consuming of all groups.
Of note, the recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks around the ligamentum arteriosum below the concavity of the arch of the aorta.
We suggest that you leave the covering pleura between the aorta and the vagus nerve well alone, and that in itself should keep the recurrent laryngeal out of harm's way.
On the other hand, all the bronchial vagal branches could be cut with impunity.
Again, there are constant bronchial arteries arising directly from the aorta.
These should be identified and secured with liger clips before dividing.
The subcarinal nodes are located at a very deep level not easily appreciated.
It is within the third tier of structures deep to the oesophagus which lies deep to the aorta.
To bring the carina up to the operating field, one could make use of retraction on the lower lobe bronchus using a strong tape.
This allows forceful traction to bring the carina into the view.
To improve exposure, the oesophagus could also be retracted using a soft vascular sloop and minimal traction.
The principle here is to follow the left main bronchus proximally until it leads us to the carina and to the right main bronchus.
The subcarinal nodes are dissected using the same technique of pinpoint diathermy, while exercising care not to injure the membranous part of the bronchus.
By the end of this dissection, the carina and the right main bronchus
as well as the posterior pericardium and the oesophagus are well on display.
Nodes within the pleural reflection of the lung are labeled as station 10, whereas the most lateral of the aortopulmonary group
at the tracheobronchial junction are labeled as station 4.
The left precoronal station 3 nodes are the most difficult to access.
They are approached below the main pulmonary artery, and therefore there is no need to cut the ligamentomartiriosum.
To expose this group of nodes, the main pulmonary artery is dissected of the bronchus, a vasculous loop passed around it, and the artery is retracted to the right-hand side in this view.
The carina is pushed down with a suction probe or a peanut on a robot,
while the pulmonary artery is pushed upwards.
This maneuver opens the precarinal space.
The main stem trachea is then clearly identified and nodal tissue in the precarinal position is dissected out.
The triangle made by the phrenic nerve and the vagus and aortic arch bears resemblance to the superior triangle on the right side, which is also limited by the same two nerves.
The consistency of the fibrofatty nodal tissue also differ here greatly and in the majority of cases it is possible to remove station 5 and 6 arm block.
The block of tissue is lifted off the main pulmonary artery into the aorto-pulmonary space.
It's important to keep dissection to the left-hand side of the vagus in this view to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The phrenic nerve could be sloped and retracted
for better dissection, but this is not mandatory.
All the tissue in the aortopulmonary space superficial to the ligamentum arteriosum is dissected and blocked if possible.
Dissection is carried over the arch up to the origin of the left subclavian artery.
Vast lobectomy is carried out if this was deferred after the systematic nodal dissection.
An extra pleural catheter is introduced.
